questions.question: Go uses 'structs' instead of classes for data grouping.
questions.explanation: Go does not have classes. It uses structs and methods to achieve object-oriented behavior.

questions.question: An interface is a collection of method signatures.
questions.explanation: A type satisfies an interface by implementing the methods defined in it.

questions.question: In Go, you can inherit properties from another struct using 'extends'.
questions.explanation: Go uses composition (struct embedding) instead of classical inheritance.

questions.question: A pointer stores the memory address of a value.
questions.explanation: Using pointers allows you to modify the original value instead of a copy.

questions.question: Go automatically handles garbage collection.
questions.explanation: Go has a built-in garbage collector that manages memory automatically.

questions.question: Goroutines are much more lightweight than OS threads.
questions.explanation: Thousands of goroutines can be multiplexed onto a small number of OS threads.
